one_line_takeaway: By treating the LLM softmax as an energy-based model, the paper shows that the logit-energy and marginal-energy terms that should mathematically cancel across consecutive decoding steps instead diverge on incorrect generations, and this training-free 'spilled energy' discrepancy detects hallucinations with better cross-dataset generalization (73.16% avg AuROC on LLaMA-3-Instruct across 9 benchmarks) than logit confidence, p(true), or trained probing classifiers evaluated out-of-distribution.
key_finding: Spilled energy with min pooling achieves the best average cross-dataset AuROC among training-free methods: 73.16% on LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct, 68.69% on base LLaMA-3, 77.49% on Mistral-7B-Instruct, and 73.94% on base Mistral (Table 1), versus the logit-confidence baseline at 54.62% (LLaMA-Instruct) and p(true) around 51-56% across models. Orgad et al.'s trained probing classifiers score higher in-distribution (e.g., mean AuROC 64.16% for LLaMA-Instruct averaged across the diagonal) but their cross-dataset (off-diagonal) performance collapses close to chance, whereas spilled energy requires no per-dataset training and generalizes more consistently (Fig. 4). Using the exact-answer-token span rather than the full sequence improves average AuROC by +24.06% for spilled energy (Min pooling) and +20.02% for marginal energy (Min), versus only +9.23% for the logit baseline (Table 2). Instruction tuning increases spilled energy's detection rate (LLaMA-3: 68.69% to 73.16%; Mistral: 73.94% to 77.49%) while it degrades the logit baseline (56.89% to 54.62% for LLaMA-3), indicating instruction-tuned models become more overconfident in raw logits but spilled energy remains a robust signal. On synthetic arithmetic, spilled energy separates correct/incorrect sums with a clear margin across all three models and all three error-difficulty tiers, with the largest advantage over logits on the hardest [1,10]-offset errors. The method also generalizes to Gemma-Instruct at 1B and 4B scale (average AuROC 75.89% and 68.67% respectively for spilled energy with min pooling, Table 3).
core_contribution: The paper reinterprets the final softmax classifier of an autoregressive LLM as an Energy-Based Model (EBM), following the discriminative-classifier-as-EBM trick of Grathwohl et al. (2020). By the chain rule of probability, the logit-derived energy at generation step i and the marginal (log-sum-exp) energy computed at step i+1 should, in principle, be mathematically equal; the paper shows they are not equal in practice and defines this discrepancy as 'spilled energy' ΔE_θ(x_i:1) (Definition 4.1, Eq. 8). It also defines a single-step 'marginal energy' E^m_θ and a combined 'scaled spilled energy' ΔE_s = |E^m_θ|·ΔE_θ. These two training-free, logit-derived metrics are proposed as hallucination/error detectors, computed only over the 'exact answer' token span (following Orgad et al. 2025's localization idea) using a pooling strategy (min pooling found best) across multi-token spans.
threat_model: None. This is not an adversarial or security paper. There is no attacker, no threat model, and no red-teaming setup. The 'errors' detected are naturally occurring LLM mistakes/hallucinations (factual errors, biased outputs, reasoning failures) on standard benchmarks and synthetic arithmetic problems, not adversarially induced or attacker-controlled failures.
limitations: The paper explicitly reports that spilled energy produces false positives on tokens that are not semantically informative -- notably punctuation (commas, periods) and sentence-initial words -- where the next-token probability mass is naturally spread across many plausible options, inflating spilled-energy values even for correct generations; this makes accurate exact-answer-token localization essential to reliable detection. It also notes markedly higher variance for marginal/spilled energy versus probing classifiers in the average column of Table 1, attributed to the metric's dependence on the LLM's intrinsic, domain-dependent energy landscape (sharp peaks in Math/HotpotQA vs. flatter distributions in Winobias/IMDB) rather than treated as a flaw. On non-instruction-aligned Mistral, spilled energy sometimes ranks slightly behind marginal energy alone. The Math dataset used for evaluation was later taken down due to a copyright claim (disclosed in the Ethics Statement); the authors state it was used prior to takedown and not redistributed.

datasets_used: Synthetic 13-digit multi-digit arithmetic sums (Easy/Medium/Hard error tiers)
quantitative_results: Spilled ΔE (Min pooling) average AuROC across 9 benchmarks: 73.16% (LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct), 68.69% (LLaMA-3-8B base), 77.49% (Mistral-7B-Instruct), 73.94% (Mistral-7B base) -- Table 1
Logit confidence average AuROC: 54.62% (LLaMA-3-Instruct), 56.89% (LLaMA-3 base), 63.44% (Mistral-Instruct), 49.51% (Mistral base) -- Table 1
p(true) baseline average AuROC: 51.29% (LLaMA-3-Instruct), 54.90% (LLaMA-3 base), 55.88% (Mistral-Instruct), 51.36% (Mistral base) -- Table 1
Orgad et al. probing classifier mean AuROC (in-distribution/diagonal average): 64.16% (LLaMA-3-Instruct), 62.84% (LLaMA-3 base), 65.56% (Mistral-Instruct), 62.11% (Mistral base) -- Table 1
Exact-answer-token localization improvement in average AuROC: Spilled ΔE (Min) +24.06%, Marginal E^m (Min) +20.02%, Marginal E^m (Max) +3.62%, Logit E^l (Max) +9.23% -- Table 2
Gemma-Instruct 4B average AuROC (Spilled ΔE, Min pooling): 75.89%; Gemma-Instruct 1B: 68.67% -- Table 3
Cross-dataset probing-classifier accuracy for Orgad et al. drops to near-chance off-diagonal (e.g., ~50-59% range) despite in-distribution diagonal values of 84-96% -- Fig. 4a
baselines_compared: Trained probing classifiers on internal representations (Orgad et al., 2025)
